title: [Bastiodon] Battle Guide
summary: A base 168/138 twin-defense [wall] that anchors a [sturdy] [stealth-rock] [lead] and converts its Defense stat into damage with [body-press].
strengths: 168 Def and 138 SpDef form the most symmetrical [wall] frame in the format
[Rock]/[Steel] typing resists nine common offensive types
weaknesses: 4x weak to [Fighting] and [Ground] — any [close-combat] or [earthquake] is an OHKO
Base 60 HP limits total damage [absorb]ed de[spite] massive defense numbers

bodyMarkdown:
## Overview
[Bastiodon] is built around one of the most extreme twin-defense spreads in the format — base 168 Def and 138 SpDef. It exists to [lead], to plant [stealth-rock] under [sturdy], and to convert its own defense stat into damage with [body-press] while the [rest] of the team rolls in behind it. Base 30 Speed is a liability on most teams but it pairs well with [trick-room]-adjacent builds that want a durable turn-one anchor.

## Strengths
[Rock]/[Steel] resists or is immune to nine common offensive types, which means [Bastiodon] comes in multiple times per game off routine pivot moves. 168 Def feeds [body-press] directly, making a unit with 52 base Attack actually threatening to physical walls and [Fighting] types. [sturdy] guarantees one full turn of action from a healthy [lead], which is the entire [stealth-rock] plan. [iron-head] covers the [Fairy] and [Ice] matchups that would otherwise force [Bastiodon] out.

## Weaknesses & Answers
The 4x weakness list is brutal: [Fighting] and [Ground] both hit for 4x, so [close-combat] or [earthquake] from any competent attacker is an automatic OHKO. Base 60 HP is the actual bulk ceiling — despite the massive defense stats, the total damage [Bastiodon] absorbs over a game is capped by that tiny HP pool. Taunt users neutralize the entire set by cutting [stealth-rock] and [toxic] in the same turn, so be ready to pivot out rather than try to trade.

## Recommended Moveset
[stealth-rock] occupies slot one — without it, [Bastiodon]'s value collapses. [body-press] is the second locked slot because it is the only way [Bastiodon] threatens actual damage; with 168 Def behind it, the move hits surprisingly hard into [Normal], [Ice], [Dark], and [Rock] walls. [iron-head] is the [Steel] STAB that punishes the Fairies and [Ice] types that try to force [Bastiodon] out. [toxic] rounds out the [wall] plan by making long games unwinnable for passive opponents, and [roar] is a legitimate alternative if the team struggles with setup sweepers.

## Item · Ability · Tera
[leftovers] is the only sensible item — every other option undercuts [Bastiodon]'s [wall]-role longevity. [sturdy] is locked in as the ability: the whole [lead] plan assumes the turn-one survival guarantee. For Tera, [Steel] is the default since it hard-removes the [Fighting] weakness and deepens [body-press] durability, and [Fairy] is the alternative when the opposing team runs dedicated [Dragon] sweepers that would bypass [Steel].

## Teammates & Synergy
[Bastiodon] needs [Psychic], [Grass], or [Flying] pivots to [absorb] the [Fighting] and [Ground] attacks it cannot switch into. After rocks go up, a physical wallbreaker or a [trick-room] cleaner should take over — [Bastiodon] cannot finish games itself. Include at least one [taunt] user on the team to answer opposing hazard removers like [defog], since the [stealth-rock] plan loses half its value if rocks are removed for free.

## Play Patterns
Lead with [Bastiodon] to guarantee turn-one [stealth-rock] under [sturdy]. If the matchup shows a [Fighting] or [Ground] threat, switch out immediately — [Bastiodon] does not trade into 4x moves under any circumstances. In longer matches, drip [toxic] into the opposing back line and [recover] through [leftovers] ticks, and only press [body-press] when a kill range is confirmed so that [Bastiodon] stays alive long enough to reset rocks if needed.
